summary: Jess Frazelle argues that automation is fundamentally about reclaiming time by transferring repetitive manual tasks to scripts and services. She presents a simple equation: time gained equals time doing the task manually minus time to automate it. The post covers her personal automations (employee onboarding in Rust, Gmail filters, newsletter management) while celebrating how consumer tools like Apple Shortcuts, home automation, and IFTTT are democratizing automation patterns for non-programmers.
key_points: Automation ROI is simple: if time to automate is less than cumulative time doing the task manually, automate it
Start by making automation work first, then make it pretty - minimize time spent on the automation itself
Consumer products like Apple Shortcuts and IFTTT are bringing developer-style automation to mass audiences
Managing configurations as code (like Gmail filters) provides version control benefits like git revert
The feeling of productivity from automation should not be exclusive to programmers
money_quotes: Time is one of the most valuable resources in the world. If there was something you could do to free more time for yourself, why wouldn't you?

I, personally, feel at my best and most productive when I am building something, solving a problem, or learning something new. In none of those situations does that include 'doing something manual that could otherwise be scripted/automated away.'
I basically automated the role of CIO in Rust.
By getting the thing to work first without wasting time on making it pretty, I find I can gain the most time and be most productive.
The feeling developers get after writing a script to make their life easier should not be exclusive.
You can imagine that most of my automations start out looking like a Rube Goldberg machine.
key_insight: Automation is fundamentally a time arbitrage calculation, and consumer tools are finally democratizing this productivity superpower beyond programmers.
